module: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: A patient with severe ARDS and refractory hypoxaemia (radial artery PaO2 45 mmHg, SaO2 81% on FiO2 100%, PEEP 15) shows no improvement despite prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade, a high-PEEP strategy, recruitment manoeuvres, iNO and HFOV. Haemodynamics are preserved (BP 115/72 mmHg, cardiac index 3.1 L/min/m², no shock).

statements.label: A
statements.text: VV-ECMO with a femoral-jugular cannulation configuration is an appropriate next step

statements.label: B
statements.text: VA-ECMO is indicated because the patient has cardiogenic shock

statements.label: C
statements.text: ECCO2R is a reasonable alternative to full ECMO given the severity of hypoxaemia

statements.label: D
statements.text: A cardiac index of 3.1 L/min/m² is consistent with a low-output, shocked state requiring haemodynamic support

questions.explanation: This patient has isolated refractory hypoxaemic respiratory failure with a preserved, non-shocked circulation (CI 3.1 L/min/m² is within a broadly normal range) — VV-ECMO, not VA-ECMO, is the appropriate configuration. ECCO2R only addresses CO2 clearance at low blood flow and provides no meaningful oxygenation support, so it is not an appropriate choice when the primary problem is refractory hypoxaemia.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: Current indications for veno-venous ECMO suggested by the Extracorporeal Life Support Organization include the following:
options: A: Hypoxemic respiratory failure (PaO2/FiO2 < 80 mm Hg), after optimal medical management, including, in the absence of contraindications, a trial of prone positioning.
B: Hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH < 7.25), despite optimal conventional mechanical ventilation (respiratory rate 35 bpm and plateau pressure [Pplat] ≤ 30 cm H2O).
C: Ventilatory support as a bridge to lung transplantation or primary graft dysfunction following lung transplant.
D: All of the above.
questions.explanation: ELSO lists all three as accepted VV-ECMO indications: refractory hypoxaemia (PaO2/FiO2<80) after optimal conventional therapy including a prone positioning trial, refractory hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH<7.25) despite optimised ventilation, and use as a bridge to lung transplantation or for primary graft dysfunction after transplant.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: The use of a single, bi-caval dual-lumen cannula in the internal jugular vein for ECLS represents an important technological advancement for patients who require a lung transplant. This is true because:
options: A: By accessing only one vessel, preferably in the upper body, it becomes possible to deliver more intensive physical therapy and rehabilitation in the pre-transplant period while on ECLS.
B: This innovative strategy may result in shorter duration of post-transplant mechanical ventilation and hospital stay, with less morbidity and mortality.
C: A + C
D: It facilitates higher rates of extracorporeal blood flow at the cost of increased recirculation.
questions.explanation: Dual-lumen cannulae reduce recirculation but the individual lumens have a lesser diameter than the usual single-lumen devices employed, so ECBF may be limited. [Källans facit-etikett för alternativ C är återgiven exakt som i originalet ("A + C") — den fullständiga referensen går inte att verifiera från skrivbilden.]
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: The major factors in determining blood CO2 removal via ECLS include all the following with the exception of:
options: A: Sweep Gas Flow
B: Surface area of oxygenator
C: Diffusibility through microfibers (could become compromised in the setting of clots or excessive fibrin deposition).
D: Hemoglobin concentration
questions.explanation: CO2 removal via the oxygenator depends on sweep gas flow, membrane surface area, and diffusibility across the microfibers (which can be impaired by clot/fibrin deposition). CO2 transport is far less dependent on haemoglobin than O2 content is, since most CO2 is carried as bicarbonate in plasma.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: The major factors in determining oxygen uptake via oxygenator include the following:
options: A: ECLS blood flow
B: Oxygen saturation of pre-oxygenator blood.
C: Hemoglobin concentration.
D: All the above.
questions.explanation: Oxygen uptake across the membrane oxygenator depends on the extracorporeal blood flow delivered, the oxygen saturation of blood entering the oxygenator, and the haemoglobin concentration, since haemoglobin determines how much oxygen the blood can carry per unit flow.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: Which are the determinants of oxygen content?
options: A: (Hb x SaO2) + (0.005 x PaO2)
B: (Hb x SaO2 x 1.5) + (0.03 x PaO2)
C: (Hb x SaO2 x 1.34) + (0.003 x PaO2)
D: (Hb x SaO2 x 1.34) + (0.0003 x PaO2)
questions.explanation: Oxygen content CaO2 = (Hb × SaO2 × 1.34) + (0.003 × PaO2), where 1.34 mL O2/g Hb is the Hüfner constant and 0.003 mL/dL/mmHg is the solubility coefficient for dissolved oxygen in plasma — matching the formula used in the case diagram.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: Which are the main applications of Extracorporeal Life Support (ECLS)?
options: A: Hypoxemia
B: Hypoxemia, hypercapnia, cardiogenic shock
C: Hypoxemia, hypercapnia, cardiogenic shock, severe acute respiratory failure
D: A+B
questions.explanation: ECLS is used for refractory hypoxaemia, refractory hypercapnia, cardiogenic shock (via V-A ECMO), and severe acute respiratory failure more broadly — its main applications extend well beyond hypoxaemia alone.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: When the origin of shock associated with respiratory failure is due to left ventricular or biventricular failure resulting in end organ hypoperfusion, which are the ECLS modality of choice?
options: A: V-V ECMO
B: V-A ECMO
C: Hybrid V-VA ECMO
D: B+C
questions.explanation: When respiratory failure is driven by left ventricular or biventricular failure with end-organ hypoperfusion, cardiac support is required in addition to gas exchange — V-A ECMO or hybrid V-VA configurations provide both, whereas V-V ECMO alone offers no haemodynamic support.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I
questions.topic: VA ECMO hybrid cardiogenic shock respiratory failure

questions.question: Which of the following sentences is correct:
options: A: Extracorporeal CO2 removal devices employ a double venous dual-lumen catheter (internal jugular or femoral vein) and low flow extracorporeal blood flow that is pumped through a gas exchanger.
B: Extracorporeal CO2 removal devices employ a single venous dual-lumen catheter (internal jugular or femoral vein) and low flow extracorporeal blood flow that is pumped through a gas exchanger.
C: Extracorporeal CO2 removal devices employ a single venous dual-lumen catheter (internal jugular or femoral vein) and high flow extracorporeal blood flow that is pumped through a gas exchanger.
D: Extracorporeal CO2 removal devices employs a double venous dual-lumen catheter (internal jugular or femoral vein) and high flow extracorporeal blood flow that is pumped through a gas exchanger.
questions.explanation: ECCO2R devices typically use a single dual-lumen venous catheter (internal jugular or femoral) with LOW extracorporeal blood flow through the gas exchanger — distinguishing them from full ECMO circuits, which use higher blood flows to also support oxygenation.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: ECCO2R reduces the volume of alveolar ventilation required to eliminate the CO2 production in COPD patients. This may theoretically enable:
options: A: A reduction of tidal volume and respiratory rate, associated with increased expiratory time, facilitating the resolution of the dynamic hyperinflation.
B: A reduction in the work of breathing despite persistent airflow limitation.
C: An increase in the work of breathing despite persistent airflow limitation
D: A and B
questions.explanation: By reducing the CO2 load requiring elimination via the lungs, ECCO2R allows a lower minute ventilation (reduced tidal volume/respiratory rate with a longer expiratory time), which both facilitates resolution of dynamic hyperinflation and reduces the work of breathing in patients with persistent airflow limitation.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: ECLS acts as an oxygenating shunt for hypoxemia refractory to conventional mechanical ventilation as a bridge to recovery. Causes of hypoxemia include the following:
options: A: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome, Acute Pulmonary Embolism, other reversible causes of acute respiratory failure, primary Graft Dysfunction following Lung Transplant.
B: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome, Acute Pulmonary Embolism, other reversible causes of acute respiratory failure, primary Graft Dysfunction following Lung Transplant, pleural effusion.

C: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome, Acute Pulmonary Embolism, other reversible causes of acute respiratory failure, primary Graft Dysfunction following Lung Transplant, pleural effusion, lung cancer.
D: All the above
questions.explanation: Recognised causes of hypoxaemic respiratory failure amenable to ECLS include ARDS, acute pulmonary embolism, other reversible causes of acute respiratory failure, and primary graft dysfunction after lung transplantation — pleural effusion and lung cancer are not listed among the core indications in this context.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: ECLS acts as an oxygenating shunt for hypoxemia refractory to conventional mechanical ventilation as a bridge to recovery. Which of the following causes of hypoxemia may benefit from ECLS?
options: A: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
B: Acute Pulmonary Embolism
C: Primary Graft Dysfunction following Lung Transplant.
D: All the above.
questions.explanation: ARDS, acute pulmonary embolism, and primary graft dysfunction following lung transplantation are all recognised, potentially reversible causes of severe hypoxaemia that may benefit from ECLS as a bridge to recovery or further therapy.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: If there is an insufficient oxygen delivery, which of the following is the more appropriate:
options: A: Optimize cardiac preload, haemoglobin, inotropy.

B: Evaluate the need to increase ECLS blood flow to achieve a target SaO2 of 88-93%. If at maximal blood flow/cannula size, evaluate for a need to insert smaller cannula to increase venous drainage (add an additional cannula) to provide more blood flow.

C: Evaluate the need to increase ECLS blood flow to achieve a target SaO2 of 88-93%. If at maximal blood flow/cannula size, evaluate for a need to insert larger cannula to increase venous drainage (add an additional cannula) to provide more blood flow.
D: A + C
questions.explanation: When oxygen delivery is insufficient, both physiological optimisation (preload, haemoglobin, inotropy) and circuit-level optimisation matter: if blood flow is limited at maximal cannula capacity, adding a LARGER (not smaller) cannula increases venous drainage and achievable extracorporeal blood flow toward the SaO2 target.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I
questions.topic: oxygen delivery optimisation ECLS blood flow

questions.question: Low flow ECCO2R, by correcting respiratory acidosis, may accomplish the goal of facilitating the resolution of dynamic lung hyperinflation. However, the most severe cases of asthma exacerbation can be characterized by hypoxemia and hemodynamic decompensation. In these conditions, it is right to say:
options: A: V-A ECMO provides the most appropriate means of support.
B: V-V ECMO or V-A-ECMO are not appropriate treatments for bronchospasm.
C: V-V ECMO is the most appropriate therapy.
D: None of the answers is correct.
questions.explanation: In severe asthma exacerbation with hypoxaemia and haemodynamic decompensation, the primary problem remains gas exchange rather than a need for circulatory support — V-V ECMO (not V-A ECMO) remains the most appropriate therapy, addressing oxygenation and CO2 clearance while avoiding the added risks of arterial cannulation.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: Which of the following can be consider the main complication of ECLS:
options: A: Air in circuit, limb ischemia, bleeding, line sepsis.
B: Air in circuit, limb ischemia, bleeding, line sepsis, clot or thrombus formation, ventilator associated pneumonia.
C: Haemolysis, deep vein thrombosis, heparin induced thrombocytopenia, respiratory muscle weakness.
D: B+C
questions.explanation: Beyond the immediate circuit complications (air in circuit, limb ischaemia, bleeding, line sepsis), ECLS carries additional risks including clot/thrombus formation, ventilator-associated pneumonia, haemolysis, deep vein thrombosis, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, and ICU-acquired weakness — the fuller complication profile spans both listed groups.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: Potential indications for use of V-A ECMO are:
options: A: Cardiogenic shock with acute fulminant myocarditis.
B: Cardiogenic shock in infective endocarditis with severe aortic regurgitation.
C: DeBakey type 1 acute aortic dissection.

D: Established multiorgan failure in a with patient decompensated dilated cardiomyopathy (recently deemed unsuitable for transplant or ventricular assist device) who has just been resuscitated from cardiac arrest.
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock due to acute fulminant myocarditis is a classic, reversible indication for V-A ECMO as a bridge to recovery. Infective endocarditis with severe aortic regurgitation, aortic dissection, and established multiorgan failure after cardiac arrest in a patient already deemed unsuitable for transplant/VAD are relative or absolute contraindications rather than indications.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I

questions.question: Regarding the ECLS circuit, which of the following sentences are the more complete:
options: A: Extracorporeal circuits use a centrifugal pump to aspirate or drain de-saturated venous blood from a large central vein; the venous blood is pumped to the oxygenator where gas exchange occurs. Oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide removal occurs across the walls of a filter.

B: Extracorporeal circuits use a centrifugal pump to aspirate or drain de-saturated venous blood from a large central vein; the venous blood is pumped to the oxygenator composed of hollow fibers where oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide removal occurs.

C: Extracorporeal circuits use a centrifugal pump to aspirate or drain de-saturated venous blood from a large central vein; the venous blood is pumped to the oxygenator composed of hollow fibers where oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide removal occurs; oxygenated and de-carboxylated blood is then returned to the patient.
D: None of the above.
questions.explanation: Modern ECLS oxygenators use hollow-fibre membranes (not a simple filter) across which gas exchange occurs; a centrifugal pump drains de-saturated venous blood from a large central vein to the oxygenator, and the oxygenated, decarboxylated blood is then returned to the patient.
questions.section: Acute Respiratory Failure ECLS Part I
